explanation: The input specifies that during the 'Final state,' all parameters (if any) must be safely stored before the final state is complete and the unit is de-energized. This means: (1) Whenever we are in the 'finalState,' parameters must be stored before leaving 'finalState' and before de-energization (represented by 'off'). The atomic propositions 'finalState', 'parametersStored', and 'off' are most relevant here. The formula encoding is: For every time the system is in 'finalState,' it must be the case that either 'parametersStored' has already happened, or it happens before either 'finalState' becomes false or 'off' becomes true (i.e., before leaving final state/turning off, parameters are stored). This is captured by the LTL formula: G(finalState -> ((!off) U parametersStored)). This says: Whenever 'finalState' holds, then as long as 'off' hasn't occurred, 'parametersStored' must eventually occur. This captures the requirement that before the system is de-energized (off) in the final state, parameters must be stored. It also prevents leaving finalState or powering off without parametersStored having occurred. Other properties, like G(finalState -> F parametersStored), would allow storing parameters after the system leaves finalState, which is incorrect. Therefore, the chosen property correctly encodes the temporal relationship implied in the requirement.

The input states that during the Final state, all parameters (if any) must be safely stored before the final state is complete and the unit is de-energized. From the atomic propositions, 'finalState' indicates being in the Final state, 'parametersStored' indicates parameters are safely stored, and 'off' indicates the system is de-energized. To capture the required order, we need to make sure that if we are in finalState, 'parametersStored' must occur before 'off' can occur (as that means the unit is de-energized and Final is over). The LTL formula F(off) -> (finalState U parametersStored) says: if the system ever turns off, then parametersStored must be true (at or before) that point, and while not yet stored, we remain in the finalState. Note that this does not allow 'off' to happen before parametersStored occurs in Final state, meeting the requirement.

The natural language states that during the Final state, all parameters must be safely stored before the Final state is complete and before the unit is de-energized. In terms of the available atomic propositions, 'finalState' denotes being in the Final state, 'parametersStored' represents parameters safely stored, and 'off' signals the system is de-energized and turned off. The correct LTL property must ensure that whenever the system is in Final state, it remains impossible for the system to transition to off (i.e., the unit is de-energized) unless parameters have already been safely stored first. This can be precisely captured by the formula: Globally, whenever Final state holds, the first time off becomes true, parametersStored must have been true before or at the same time. This is the classic 'parametersStored happens before or at the same time as off while in finalState'. LTL expresses this as G(finalState -> (!off U (parametersStored & X(off | (!off U parametersStored))))). However, a simpler and equivalent way is to say that whenever off happens during finalState, parametersStored must be true at that moment or earlier. Thus, G((finalState & off) -> parametersStored) ensures that you cannot be in finalState and off without parametersStored having become true. This satisfies the requirement, avoids numeric comparison operators, and only uses the given atomic propositions. Alternative formulas could overconstrain or underconstrain by, for instance, not enforcing the ordering. This property solves the problem directly and concisely.

The natural language requirement states that while in the Final state, all parameters (if any) must be safely stored before the Final state is complete and the unit is de-energized (off). In the atomic propositions, 'finalState' indicates being in the Final state, 'parametersStored' indicates that parameters have been safely stored, and 'off' indicates the system is de-energized. The requirement is that, always during the Final state, before transitioning to 'off', 'parametersStored' must occur. In LTL, this is captured by stipulating that within 'finalState', it is always the case that if 'off' becomes true (i.e., the system is de-energized), then somewhere earlier in the same 'finalState', 'parametersStored' has already occurred. This can be formally stated as: globally, when in 'finalState', it must never happen that 'off' occurs before 'parametersStored'. In LTL, we express this as: 'Within 'finalState', it is always the case that either it is not yet 'off', or, if it is 'off', then 'parametersStored' has previously occurred.' This is encoded as: G(finalState -> ((!off) U parametersStored) | G(!off)), or equivalently (if you want to ensure 'parametersStored' always occurs before 'off' within 'finalState'): G(finalState -> (parametersStored V !off)). The 'release' (V) operator enforces that 'parametersStored' must become true at least by the time 'off' becomes true; if 'off' never comes, the formula is vacuously true. This matches the stated requirement.

The natural language states that during the 'Final state', all parameters must be safely stored before the final state completes and before the unit is de-energized. From the list of atomic propositions, 'finalState' indicates the system is in the Final state, 'parametersStored' means parameters have been safely stored, and 'off' indicates the unit is de-energized.

To capture the requirement, we want to say: Whenever we are in the 'Final state', 'parametersStored' must become true before 'off' (i.e., before the unit is de-energized). This can be formulated in LTL as: G(finalState -> (!off U parametersStored)). This means: Globally, whenever we are in 'Final state', it must always be the case that 'off' does not occur until 'parametersStored' has occurred, i.e., parameters are stored before power-off. This is the closest faithful mapping given the available atomic propositions.

Any previous LTL formulations like G(finalState -> parametersStored) would not be sufficient, since they require 'parametersStored' to always hold during 'Final state', but do not enforce that it occurs before de-energization (off). Our formula also does not constrain when the final state ends, but enforce the required ordering relative to 'off'.

The requirement states that during the Final state, all parameters must be safely stored before the final state is complete and the unit is de-energized. In terms of the available atomic propositions: 'finalState' indicates that we are in the Final state, 'parametersStored' indicates parameters have been stored, and 'off' indicates the system is completely de-energized. To express 'all parameters are to be safely stored before ... final state is complete and the unit is de-energized,' we must ensure that parametersStoried occurs before off, and this must hold whenever we are in the Final state. The property 'G(finalState -> (!off U parametersStored))' captures that if the system is in Final state, then until parameters are stored, off cannot occur (so off is prevented until after parameters are stored). This ensures parametersStored happens before off, during all times we are in finalState, which matches the requirement. Other approaches might fail to capture the need for storing parameters before off in every instance of the Final state context.

The requirement says that during the 'Final state', all parameters must be safely stored before the final state completes and the unit is de-energized (off). Translating this, we note that 'finalState' should hold, and before 'off' becomes true (i.e., system de-energized), 'parametersStored' must have occurred. In LTL, the canonical way to express this is: whenever we are in the finalState, it must always be the case that until we reach 'off', we ensure that 'parametersStored' becomes true before 'off'. Thus: globally, whenever we're in finalState, it is required that parametersStored holds at or before the transition to 'off'. The property is captured with: G(finalState -> (!off U (parametersStored | off))). This ensures that if we're in finalState and reach off, then parametersStored must be true at or before that time. It does not strictly require parametersStored before every instant of finalState, but guarantees safe storage before finalization. Alternative LTLs that do not use 'U' or that simply require G(finalState -> parametersStored) do not capture the idea that safe storage is before de-energize (off); whereas this property explicitly relates the ordering with 'until'.

The natural language specifies that during the 'Final state' (finalState), before the unit is de-energized (off), all parameters must be safely stored (parametersStored). So, for all points while in the final state, before transitioning to being off, parameters should be stored. This is best captured as: globally, whenever we are in the final state, it must be the case that until we reach 'off' (de-energized), the parameters have been stored. That is, after entering Final State, and before reaching Off, at every moment either parameters have already been stored by that point, or we remain in finalState. This is written as G(finalState -> (parametersStored V off)), which means: whenever in the Final state, parameters must have been stored at least by the time we de-energize ('off' becomes true) (meaning it's not allowed to pass through finalState into off without parametersStored being true).

The natural language statement specifies that during the Final state, all parameters (if any) are to be safely stored BEFORE the final state is considered complete and BEFORE the unit is de-energized (i.e., turned completely off). This means that, once the finalState is entered, parametersStored should happen before off, and finalState does not 'end' until both have happened. In LTL, the guarantee required is that: whenever we are in finalState, before the system becomes off (de-energized), parametersStored must have already occurred. This is captured by universally quantifying over the time in finalState and stating that: while in finalState, if we are not yet off, then either parametersStored has already happened, or it will happen before off occurs. This is most succinctly captured as: globally, whenever finalState holds, it is always the case that parameters are stored before off is reached (i.e., until off occurs, parametersStored must occur). This gives the formula: G(finalState -> (!off U parametersStored)).

The natural language constraint states that during the Final state, all parameters must be safely stored before the final state completes and the unit is de-energized. The atomic propositions available are 'finalState' indicating the system is in the Final state, 'parametersStored' indicating parameters are safely stored, and 'off' indicating the system is de-energized. To capture the temporal sequence requirement (store parameters before powering down, while in the Final state), we need to ensure that at any time while in finalState, if the system will be de-energized (off) in the future while still in the Final state, then 'parametersStored' must have occurred beforehand in the Final state. This is classically expressed in LTL as: Globally, if we are in finalState and will eventually turn off while still in finalState, then before that 'off', 'parametersStored' must have occurred (while still in finalState). We express 'parametersStored' occurring before 'off' within the same Final state as 'finalState -> ((!(off) & finalState) U (off & parametersStored & finalState))', but since the requirement is stricter (every 'off' in Final must be preceded by 'parametersStored' in Final without intervening exit from 'finalState'), the correct LTL is: G((finalState & X((finalState U (off & finalState)))) -> ((!(off & finalState)) U (parametersStored & finalState))). This ensures that for every run of 'finalState', until 'off & finalState' occurs, 'parametersStored & finalState' has held at least once. Alternatively, commonly in safety properties, we specify that it is never the case that the system transitions to both 'off' and 'finalState' (i.e., de-energized in Final) without 'parametersStored' having occurred beforehand in Final. The strongest and most directly-encoded version given available propositions is: 'G((finalState & off) -> (parametersStored & finalState))', which ensures that whenever the system is both off and in Final state, the parameters have been safely stored in Final state. This is a direct and safe translation given the available atomic propositions.
